---
title: "Tagma"
subtitle: "A Content-Addressable Hardware Primitive Defined by the Hangul Syllabic Coordinate Space"
metadata-files:
  - ./_include/author.yml
  - ./_include/format.pdf.yml
  - ./_include/format.html.yml
format:
  html: {}
  pdf: {}
---

{{< include ./_include/_title_meta_items.qmd >}}

```{python}
#| include: false
#| context: local
%run ./_include/_graphviz.py
```

## Introduction

Project Tagma defines a hardware primitive for computing derived from the coordinate system embedded in Hangul syllables. The Unicode standard allocates a contiguous block of 11,172 complete Hangul syllables, each encoded as a fixed 16-bit value. Tagma treats these syllables as coordinates in a three-dimensional space. This coordinate space provides a structural identity primitive: a 16-bit value whose composition is its own identifier, requiring no hash function, no lookup table, and no collision handling.

A coordinate can be displayed as a Hangul syllable for debugging, decomposed into its three axis values for assembly, or processed as a raw 16-bit value by the hardware. The Projector layer that performs these translations is a software interface and is not part of the decoder specification.

Tagma is not a language, an encoding standard, or an instruction set architecture. It is a specification for a silicon block that occupies a specific layer: the boundary between binary representation and content-addressable identity. It exists because current systems generate identity through hash functions that are expensive in gates, cycles, and storage, and because the Hangul coordinate space happens to provide a closed-form deterministic alternative.

## Historical Foundation: The 1443 Design

Hangul was created in 1443 under a documented scientific and philosophical design principle, recorded in the Hunminjeongeum Haerye. The document explicitly describes how consonants are derived from articulator shapes and vowels from heaven-earth-human triadic symbolism. This design was not an arbitrary encoding choice but a structural analysis of the Korean phonological system, systematically decomposed into components that combine to form syllables through a fixed composition rule.

The Unicode syllable composition formula implements this 600-year-old design in a modern encoding standard:

```text
code_point = 0xAC00 + (initial x 588) + (medial x 28) + final
```

The formula exists because the original 1443 design was structural: a small set of components combining through a fixed rule to generate a complete syllable space. This is what distinguishes Hangul from every other writing system encoded in Unicode. The structural property that makes Tagma possible was built into the script at its creation.

Tagma is not imposing a computational structure onto a linguistic artifact. It is using a structure that already existed.

## Philosophical Grounding: Structure Before Name

In current computing, data is identified by hash — a derived value that depends on the data but is not the data itself. The identifier is separate from the structure it identifies. This indirection has costs: generation time, collision probability, lookup infrastructure.

Tagma proceeds from a different principle: the coordinate is the structure; the structure is the identifier. A syllable coordinate is not a label assigned to a piece of data. It is a decomposition of the data into three axes whose combined values uniquely determine the coordinate, and the coordinate uniquely determines the combined values. There is no gap between what something is and what it is called.

This is not a claim about linguistic philosophy. It is a description of how the Hangul composition formula works. The formula produces a one-to-one mapping between every valid syllable and its 16-bit coordinate. The coordinate does not reference the syllable; it is the syllable, expressed as a triple of structural fields. Tagma extends this property to computing: a 16-bit value whose structural composition is its identity.

## The End of ASCII: A Historical Transition

ASCII was a 7-bit encoding designed for teleprinters in 1963. It was a product of contingent history: the 7-bit width was chosen to fit within the 8-bit byte used by early data transmission equipment, leaving one bit per byte permanently unused. The script it encoded was Latin, which has no structural composition formula. Every character is an atomic assignment.

The Unicode standard inherited this foundation. It extended ASCII to cover the world's scripts, but it could not undo the structural properties of the scripts themselves. Latin has no formula. Hanzi has no formula. Hangul has one because it was designed with one.

Tagma uses this structural anomaly to define a hardware primitive that the existing encoding space does not provide. The transition from 7-bit ASCII to 16-bit structured coordinates is not an incremental improvement. It is a change in kind: from arbitrary assignment to formulaic composition, from variable-width encoding to fixed-width structural verification, from hash-based identity to structure-based identity.

## The Encoding Problem

Variable-length encoding has architectural consequences that are independent of any particular implementation. Memory alignment is not guaranteed. Instruction decoding must detect boundary positions. Prefetch and pipeline efficiency are affected. Hardware-level structural validation cannot rely on fixed field decomposition. These are structural properties of variable-length design, not engineering limitations.

```{python}
#| label: fig-encoding
#| fig-cap: "Encoding hierarchy from variable-length to fixed-length structural encoding."
dot("""
digraph EncodingHierarchy {
    rankdir=TB;
    node [shape=box];
    binary [label="Binary / Transistor"];
    ascii [label="ASCII (1963)\n7-bit, variable"];
    unicode [label="Unicode (ISO 10646)\nmultiple encoding forms"];
    utf8 [label="UTF-8\n1-4 byte variable"];
    utf16 [label="UTF-16\n2-byte + surrogate"];
    tagma [label="Tagma\n16-bit fixed\nclosed-form formula\nstructural validity"];
    binary -> ascii;
    binary -> unicode;
    unicode -> utf8;
    unicode -> utf16;
    unicode -> tagma [label="  Hangul block only"];
}
""")
```

| Encoding | Storage unit | Addressable symbols | Waste per unit | Fixed alignment | Structural HW check |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|----------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| ASCII | 1 byte (8 bits) | 128 of 256 | 1 bit (12.5%) | yes | no |
| Latin-1 | 1 byte | 191 of 256 | variable | yes | no |
| UTF-8 (English) | 1-4 bytes | unlimited | none for ASCII | no | no |
| UTF-16 (BMP) | 2 bytes | 63,488 of 65,536 | 2,048 reserved | no | no |
| UTF-32 | 4 bytes | unlimited | 75-88% for common use | yes | no |
| **Tagma** | **2 bytes** | **11,172 of 65,536** | **none** | **yes** | **yes (54,364 invalid)** |

The structural constraint in Tagma arises from the fixed formula by which Hangul syllables are composed. Of the 65,536 states representable in 16 bits, only 11,172 satisfy the composition rules. A hardware decoder can distinguish valid from invalid states using combinational logic derived from the Unicode composition formula. No lookup table, no software intervention, and no additional storage is required.

## The Identity Problem

Every system that stores data by content rather than by location must generate identifiers. Current approaches and their hardware cost:

| Method | Identifier size | Generation cost | Collision | Lookup structure |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|-----------|------------------|
| Pointer | 32-64 bits | zero | none | direct (location-based) |
| Hash (SHA-256) | 256 bits | ~10K gates, 64-75 cycles | probabilistic | hash table + resolution |
| UUID | 128 bits | entropy-dependent | probabilistic | hash table |
| CAM | per-bit comparison | 9-16 transistors/bit | none | associative |
| **Tagma** | **16 bits** | **~300 gates, 1 cycle** | **none (formulaic)** | **direct (coordinate = address)** |

Hash-based identity requires a hardware SHA-256 engine, a hash table with collision resolution, and additional storage for 32-byte identifiers. Tagma replaces all of this with a combinational decoder and a 16-bit register. The trade-off is that Tagma identifiers are limited to 11,172 distinct values.

## The Hangul Coordinate Space

This limit of 11,172 arises from the structure of the Unicode Hangul syllable block. The standard defines a contiguous range from U+AC00 to U+D7AF where each syllable is generated by a fixed formula:

```text
code_point = 0xAC00 + (initial x 588) + (medial x 28) + final
```

where `initial` (0-18), `medial` (0-20), and `final` (0-27) are zero-based indices. The three axes compose to 19 x 21 x 28 = 11,172 valid syllables, stored within a 16-bit space of 65,536 possible values. The remaining 54,364 states are structurally invalid.

```{python}
#| label: fig-hangul-axes
#| fig-cap: "The three structural axes of the Hangul syllable coordinate space."
dot("""
digraph HangulAxes {
    rankdir=LR;
    node [shape=box];
    syllable [label="Hangul syllable\n16-bit coordinate\nU+AC00..U+D7AF", shape=box];
    initial [label="Initial\n19 values\n5 bits"];
    medial [label="Medial\n21 values\n5 bits"];
    final [label="Final\n28 values\n5 bits"];
    syllable -> initial [label="  divide by 588"];
    syllable -> medial [label="  divide by 28 (remainder)"];
    syllable -> final [label="  mod 28"];
}
""")
```

| Field | Bits | Values | Range |
|-------|------|--------|-------|
| Initial (choseong) | 5 | 19 | 0-18 |
| Medial (jungseong) | 5 | 21 | 0-20 |
| Final (jongseong) | 5 | 28 | 0-27 |
| Reserved | 1 | -- | -- |

This coordinate space was established through sustained technical work by Korean standards experts who demonstrated that Hangul is a compositional system expressible as a closed-form formula, leading to its adoption in Unicode 2.0. It is structurally unique in Unicode. No other script satisfies all four conditions that make Tagma possible: a fixed closed-form composition formula, complete fixed-width 16-bit representation without surrogate pairs or variable-length escape sequences, a structural invalidity margin of 54,364 states for hardware-level detection, and an open international standard (ISO/IEC 10646) with no proprietary encumbrance. Latin has fixed width but no formula: each letter is individually assigned, not generated by a structural rule. Hanzi has no formula: radicals and phonetics combine irregularly with no closed-form code point mapping. Arabic has neither formula nor fixed width: contextual shaping changes glyph form. Only Hangul satisfies all four conditions.

## Identity Without Hash: The Core Technical Value

Tagma replaces the identifier generation pipeline (hash function, collision resolution, lookup table) with a direct mapping: the coordinate IS the identifier. The comparison is quantitative:

| Property | SHA-256 based | Tagma | Factor |
|----------|-------------|-------|--------|
| Identifier size | 256 bits | 16 bits | 16x smaller |
| Generation latency | 64-75 cycles | 1 cycle (combinational) | 64-75x lower |
| Collision | probabilistic, requires resolution | deterministic, zero | logic eliminated |
| Hardware for generation | ~10,000 gates (SHA-256 engine) | ~300 gates (decoder) | ~30x fewer gates |
| Lookup structure | hash table + collision list | direct address (coordinate = index) | entire structure eliminated |
| Maximum identifiers | unlimited | 11,172 | constrained |

The boundary is clear: Tagma addresses the class of content-addressable storage with up to approximately 11,000 entries. Systems in this range gain the efficiency of direct-mapped content addressability without CAM cell overhead or hash logic. Systems outside this range require the hash path.

## The Decoder: A Combinational Block

The decoder extracts three 5-bit fields from a 16-bit input in three combinational stages:

```{python}
#| label: fig-decoder
#| fig-cap: "Three-stage decoder flow: range check, field extraction, validation."
dot("""
digraph DecoderFlow {
    rankdir=LR;
    node [shape=box];
    input [label="16-bit input"];
    range [label="Range\nU+AC00..U+D7AF"];
    extract [label="Field extraction\noffset / 588 / 28"];
    validate [label="Validation\ninitial<19 medial<21 final<28"];
    output [label="valid + 3 fields"];
    invalid [label="invalid flag", shape=box];
    input -> range;
    range -> extract [label="in range"];
    range -> invalid [label="out of range"];
    extract -> validate;
    validate -> output [label="pass"];
    validate -> invalid [label="fail"];
}
""")
```

**Stage 1: Range check.** Two comparators verify the input is within [U+AC00, U+D7AF]. An out-of-range value produces an immediate invalid flag.

**Stage 2: Field extraction.** For in-range values:

```text
offset = input - 0xAC00
initial = offset / 588     # integer division by constant (synthesized as multiply-shift)
remainder = offset % 588
medial = remainder / 28     # integer division by constant
final = remainder % 28
```

**Stage 3: Validation.** Three comparators check: initial < 19, medial < 21, final < 28. Any failure sets the invalid flag.

Tagma coordinates support proximity-based search without dedicated search hardware. Given two coordinates, the field-wise Hamming distance (initial distance, medial distance, final distance) is computed by three banks of parallel XOR gates. This is a purely combinational operation requiring no clock cycles. The three distance values serve as a similarity metric: the coordinate pair with minimum aggregate distance is the nearest structural neighbor. This enables associative lookup without hash tables, B-trees, or CAM cells. For example, a query coordinate can be compared against an array of stored coordinates in parallel, returning the nearest match in the same cycle. The three distance values (initial, medial, final) can be weighted independently to prioritize specific structural axes.

No lookup tables, no dividers (division by constant is synthesized as multiply-shift), no state.

The decoder changes the nature of the operation. In conventional silicon, a value is read and passed to an arithmetic unit that performs a fixed operation such as addition or multiplication. In the Tagma decoder, a value is read and decomposed into structural fields. The operation is not arithmetic transformation but structural decomposition. Computation shifts from numeric to structural: the three extracted fields are not intermediate results but the intrinsic composition of the value itself.

| Component | Gates |
|-----------|-------|
| Range check (U+AC00, U+D7AF) | ~50 |
| Offset subtraction | ~80 |
| Division by 588 (multiply-shift) | ~80 |
| Division by 28 (multiply-shift) | ~60 |
| Validation comparators x3 | ~30 |
| **Total** | **~300** |

Approximately 300 gate equivalents in 28nm. The decoder is smaller than a single 32-bit multiplier.

## Part I: Coprocessor Implementation (Current)

### Integration Path

In a conventional von Neumann architecture, instruction memory and data memory are logically separated. Tagma defines a unified 16-bit coordinate space where a coordinate serves as a memory address, an instruction opcode, or a state identifier. The distinction is a matter of interpretation applied at the moment of observation, not of the coordinate's location in separate memory regions. Fetching a coordinate is a single memory operation. The program counter is a coordinate value that transitions based on state and scheme rather than advancing through a separate instruction space.

Tagma attaches to a RISC-V pipeline through the XIF coprocessor interface. This is a standard interface that allows custom instructions without modifying the core pipeline.

```{python}
#| label: fig-stack
#| fig-cap: "Tagma position in the system stack."
dot("""
digraph TagmaStack {
    rankdir=TB;
    node [shape=box];
    app [label="Application logic"];
    riscv [label="RISC-V pipeline\n(unmodified)"];
    interface [label="XIF coprocessor interface", shape=box];
    tagma [label="Tagma decoder\n~300 gates, 1 cycle"];
    mem [label="Standard memory hierarchy\n(unmodified)"];
    app -> riscv;
    riscv -> interface [style=dashed];
    interface -> tagma;
    tagma -> mem;
}
""")
```

The three custom instructions are:

**tagma_check rd, rs1.** Decodes the lower 16 bits of rs1. Returns a 16-bit value: valid flag (1 bit, 1 = valid), reserved (1 bit, reads as zero), initial (5 bits), medial (5 bits), final (5 bits). Invalid inputs produce a zero valid flag and undefined field values. One cycle, combinational.

**tagma_dist rd, rs1, rs2.** Computes field-wise Hamming distance between the two 16-bit coordinates. Returns three 5-bit distances (initial, medial, final). One cycle, combinational.

**tagma_mux rd, rs1, rs2, imm5.** If the selected field (imm5, where 0=initial, 1=medial, 2=final) of rs1 and rs2 are equal, rd = rs1; else rd = rs2. The immediate operand is encoded in the instruction word according to the XIF specification for 5-bit immediates.

### Verification

The coordinate space is exhaustively enumerable: 65,536 values, of which 11,172 are valid. A verification harness generates all 65,536 inputs, applies the decoder specification, and records the results. The enumeration engine is the same one used for RISC-V instruction space verification; only the constraint set changes. The bijection is verified by enumeration: no collisions, no unassigned values within the block.

### Architectural Mapping to SSCCS Primitives

Tagma maps to SSCCS structural primitives ([SSCCS Specification](https://docs.ssccs.org)). A coordinate in this space is a Segment: a 16-bit value whose identity is its structure. The composition rules of initial, medial, and final correspond to the Scheme that defines which coordinates are valid. Validity predicates (range checks) implement the Field by defining admissible coordinate ranges. Enumerating coordinates under constraints implements Observation: a hardware-level selection of valid states. Tagma makes these abstract primitives directly executable in silicon.

### Comparison with Existing Paradigms

**vs. CAM.** CAM provides content-based lookup in one cycle but at 9-16 transistors per bit (vs. 6 for SRAM). At the Tagma maximum of 11,172 entries:

| Approach | Cells | Decoders |
|----------|-------|----------|
| Linear SRAM (11,172 x 16-bit) | 178,752 | 1 |
| CAM (11,172 x 16-bit) | ~446,880 | 0 |
| Tagma 3D (19 x 21 x 28 x 16-bit) | 178,752 | 3 small |

CAM is the most area-efficient when all entries are densely used. Tagma with direct mapping uses standard SRAM cells at 6 transistors per bit, approximately 2.5x less than CAM, at the cost of 54,364 unused states. Tagma is more area-efficient when the stored set is sparse; CAM is more efficient when dense.

**vs. Hash-Based Lookup.** Quantified above. Tagma replaces the entire hash pipeline. The trade-off is identifier count versus gate/cycle/storage cost.

**vs. Linear Memory.** Pointer-based addressing has zero generation cost and no collision, but provides no content-addressability. The relationship between stored content and its address is arbitrary. Tagma provides content-addressability at the hardware level, with the address derived from structure rather than location.

**vs. Fixed-Length RISC Instructions.** RISC-V solved the variable-length instruction decode problem with 32-bit fixed-length encoding. Tagma applies the same principle to data identifiers: fixed-length, self-decodable, structurally verifiable. The two are complementary.

## Part II: Memory Hierarchy Penetration (Future)

The coprocessor path (Part I) attaches Tagma to a linear memory system. The coordinate is decoded and the result is used as an address in conventional memory. The three stages below describe progressively deeper integration.

### Stage 2: Memory Controller Mapping

The Tagma coordinate is used directly as the physical address in the memory controller, bypassing virtual-to-physical address translation for the Tagma address space. The controller maps `initial-medial-final` to `row-column` within the existing memory array. This is a change to the memory controller logic, not to the bitcell array. Feasibility: existing RISC-V memory controllers accept custom address mapping logic.

### Stage 3: Three-Dimensional Decode

The bitcell array itself is laid out with three independent decode dimensions. Instead of a linear N-bit address decoded into row and column, the three coordinate fields each have their own decoder:

```text
initial[4:0] → initial decoder → primary wordline
medial[4:0]  → medial decoder  → secondary wordline
final[4:0]   → final decoder   → column select
```

The bitcell array can be organized as a 19 x 21 x 28 grid (11,172 cells) rather than a linear array. Alternatively, a memory controller can map the 11,172 valid coordinates to a dense linear array through a simple packed-index transformation, eliminating empty address space at the cost of one additional mapping layer. The choice between sparse 3D decode and dense linear mapping depends on whether decode simplicity or physical area is the constraint. Each coordinate field selects independently. This is not a new memory cell technology; it is a reorganization of the decoder topology. The bitcell array itself remains standard 6T SRAM. The change is in the peripheral circuitry: three independent decoders replace the single row-column decoder pair, and the sense amplifier layout must accommodate three-axis access. This increases peripheral complexity but leaves the bitcell array unchanged. One linear decoder becomes three decoders (initial, medial, final), each smaller.

| Parameter | Linear (SRAM) | Tagma 3D (SRAM) |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| Decoder type | 1 decoder (N-to-2^N) | 3 decoders (5-to-19, 5-to-21, 5-to-28) |
| Worst-case decoder delay | O(2^N) | O(32) max (5-bit decode) |
| Address mapping | row + column | initial + medial + final |
| Address translation | required (virtual → physical) | none (coordinate = array index) |
| Structural validity | external (ECC) | built-in (decoder rejects out-of-range fields) |

The three small decoders (19, 21, 28 ways) are each significantly smaller than a single large decoder for an equivalent linear array. The worst-case decode path is a 5-input decoder (32 possible outputs), regardless of array size.

### Current Status

The decoder specification and coordinate space definition are complete. Part I (XIF coprocessor) is the current implementation path. The decoder logic, coordinate space specification, and verification methodology follow the same pattern as the existing verification pipeline. Part II (memory controller mapping and 3D decoder layout) is specified but not yet implemented. The two paths share the identical coordinate specification and field decomposition; they differ only in the layer at which decode occurs.

## Application Domains

The deterministic single-cycle decode makes Tagma suitable for real-time and safety-critical systems. The three-decoder topology of the future path reduces worst-case decode latency compared to a single linear decoder. The structural error trapping is relevant for high-radiation environments where a baseline validity check supplements ECC. Proximity-based search (Hamming distance across three fields) supports pattern matching and nearest-neighbor lookup without specialized hardware.

## Boundaries

A single-bit error that maps one valid coordinate to another is not detected by the decoder. At the architectural level, such a transition can be interpreted as a structural state change within the valid space rather than an error: the coordinate has moved to an adjacent valid state. Whether this constitutes corruption or transformation depends on the constraints applied at the observation layer, not on the decoder itself. External integrity checks at the system level determine which transitions are admissible.

Tagma does not replace general-purpose instruction sets or memory architectures. It provides a content-addressable identity primitive for systems whose working set fits within 11,172 entries. Part I is compatible with existing RISC-V pipelines and verification flows. Part II is a future direction with no current implementation timeline. The structural validity check provided by the decoder does not eliminate the need for full error-correcting codes: single-bit errors that map one valid coordinate to another are not detected.

## Vision

Tagma is a small silicon block with a precise boundary: 11,172 identifiers, ~300 gates, one clock cycle. It does not replace general-purpose computing. It provides a foundation for content-addressable systems that the current hash-based approach cannot economically reach at this gate count and latency. The coordinate space it uses happens to be Hangul, not because of any cultural preference, but because no other Unicode script provides the closed-form three-axis structure that makes a combinational decoder possible. This is not a claim about linguistic superiority; it is a statement about the mathematical properties of an existing international standard.
 
{{< include ./_include/_license.md >}}
